Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Online Methods lists pdr9–2 as affecting At3g53280.

Source_Species.reason: The study gene work is in Arabidopsis thaliana mutants in the Col-0 background unless otherwise noted.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Online Methods states all mutants are in the Col-0 background unless otherwise noted; pdr9–2 is not noted otherwise.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The experiments on PDR9 were performed in Arabidopsis thaliana.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: pdr9–2 is in Col-0 background per Methods.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results describe pdr9–2 as a mutant defective in export of coumarins from roots; PDR9 is a known pleiotropic drug resistance/ABC transporter, consistent with the claim though not elaborated here.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results directly state sideretin in spent medium is ~10-fold lower in pdr9–2.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Sideretin is described as a coumarin derived from fraxetin and from feruloyl-CoA, i.e. phenylpropanoid-derived specialized metabolite.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Paper identifies sideretin as the primary molecule exuded under iron deficiency and shows catecholic coumarins mobilize iron and support uptake.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Results state sideretin is found in spent medium and sideretin glucosides are elevated in roots of pdr9–2.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Directly supported: PDR9 is the principal sideretin root exporter; pdr9–2 has ~10-fold lower sideretin in exudates, elevated root sideretin glucosides, and pdr9–2 is more chlorotic/stunted with lower chlorophyll and Fe at high pH.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: PDR9 effects were studied under iron deficiency; growth phenotype appears when iron availability is decreased by high-pH conditions.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Supported by mutant metabolite profiling of spent medium and roots plus soil and agar phenotyping under altered Fe availability.

Omics_Data.reason: Comparative metabolomics by HPLC-ESI-MS/LC-MS is explicitly described; no accession number is provided.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results show pdr9–2 mutants are more chlorotic and stunted and have lower chlorophyll and Fe concentrations under alkaline low-availability iron conditions.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper does not explicitly discuss PDR9 as an engineering target or recommend stacking with biosynthetic genes.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results explicitly state spent medium sideretin is approximately 10-fold lower in pdr9–2 and root sideretin glucosides are elevated.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summary points are directly supported by Results: principal exporter role, reduced exudate/increased root glucosides, and impaired iron acquisition phenotype under challenging conditions.

corrections.old_value: Important target for improving secretion of beneficial Fe-mobilizing coumarins; likely should be stacked with biosynthetic genes
corrections.reason: The paper does not explicitly discuss PDR9 as an engineering target or recommend stacking with biosynthetic genes.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Online Methods lists f6’h1 lines as At3g13610.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Mutants are in Col-0 background unless otherwise noted; f6’h1 is not excepted.

Applied_Species.reason: Core mutant studies are in Arabidopsis.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: This paper does not explicitly state F6’H1 is a 2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase, though S8H is identified as a 2-ODD with 48% identity to F6’H1 and prior work is cited.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: F6’H1 is placed upstream in the sideretin/catecholic coumarin biosynthetic pathway from feruloyl-CoA to scopoletin and downstream products.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Results show F6’H1 expression in N. benthamiana yields scopoletin, establishing the scopoletin-forming branch entry.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: F6’H1 is clearly upstream of S8H and CYP82C4.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Results and pathway figure description state the pathway begins from feruloyl-CoA and F6’H1 is responsible for scopoletin biosynthesis.

Primary_Product.reason: Expression of F6’H1 alone results in accumulation of scopoletin.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Supported by the enzyme name feruloyl-CoA 6’-hydroxylase and by the statement that F6’H1 is responsible for scopoletin biosynthesis from feruloyl-CoA.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: F6’H1 supplies the upstream precursor pool for fraxetin/sideretin biosynthesis.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Loss of F6’H1 abolishes scopoletin-derived downstream metabolites including fraxetin and sideretin.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Fraxetin and sideretin are coumarins and are described as deriving from feruloyl-CoA.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Catecholic coumarins are shown to mobilize Fe(III) in vitro and mediate iron uptake in vivo.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Exudates and root glucosides are described for pathway metabolites.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results show f6’h1 abolishes the pathway metabolites in exudates and causes impaired iron nutrition under high pH/low Fe availability.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper does not claim rate limitation, but it does support that F6’H1 is a required pathway-entry enzyme for scopoletin-derived coumarin biosynthesis.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: This paper references prior work for F6’H1 expression and states F6’H1 is also expressed in cortex; however it does not itself present direct qPCR/promoter analysis here for F6’H1 upregulation in iron-deficient roots.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Same issue: likely true from cited prior work, but not directly shown for F6’H1 in this paper’s Results.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: F6’H1-related phenotypes and metabolite changes were tested under iron deficiency and elevated pH.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Supported by T-DNA knockout metabolite profiling, transient expression in N. benthamiana, rescue with exogenous coumarins, and alkaline soil/agar phenotyping.

Omics_Data.reason: Comparative metabolomics is explicit; transcriptome mining sources are named and no accession is given.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: The paper does not report physical protein-protein interactions between F6’H1, S8H, and CYP82C4; they are pathway-related, not shown interactors.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: f6’h1 mutants show chlorosis and reduced chlorophyll/Fe under alkaline low-iron conditions.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper discusses broad hopes for engineering improved iron acquisition but does not specifically nominate F6’H1 with the stated stacking recommendation.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results explicitly state F6’H1 expression alone in N. benthamiana produced scopoletin and also esculetin, and f6’h1 mutants lacked the major oxidized coumarins in exudates.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summary statements are supported by mutant, transient expression, and phenotype data.

corrections.reason: The paper does not report physical protein-protein interactions between F6’H1, S8H, and CYP82C4; they are pathway-related, not shown interactors.

corrections.old_value: Candidate for engineering improved iron acquisition by enabling catecholic coumarin biosynthesis, but likely should be combined with downstream biosynthetic/export components
corrections.reason: The paper discusses broad hopes for engineering improved iron acquisition but does not specifically nominate F6’H1 with the stated stacking recommendation.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Results and Methods identify S8H as At3g12900.

Source_Species.reason: S8H is an Arabidopsis thaliana gene.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Mutants are Col-0 background unless otherwise noted; s8h is not excepted.

Applied_Species.reason: Core functional tests are in Arabidopsis.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana was used for mutant and reporter analyses.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: s8h lines are in Col-0 background.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Results state At3g12900 functions as a scopoletin 8-hydroxylase (S8H).

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results identify At3g12900 as a gene encoding a 2-ODD.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: S8H is part of the sideretin/catecholic coumarin biosynthetic pathway.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: S8H performs the fraxetin-forming step from scopoletin.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: S8H acts between F6’H1 and CYP82C4, i.e. midstream.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Mutants exude scopoletin but not further oxidized coumarins, and F6’H1+S8H expression yields fraxetin, supporting scopoletin as substrate.

Primary_Product.reason: Fraxetin accumulates when F6’H1 and S8H are coexpressed.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results explicitly conclude At3g12900 functions as scopoletin 8-hydroxylase to form fraxetin.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: Fraxetin is presented as the direct precursor to sideretin.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: S8H is required for formation of fraxetin and downstream sideretin.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: These are coumarins derived in the sideretin pathway.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Catecholic coumarins are shown to solubilize/reduce Fe(III) and support iron uptake.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Pathway metabolites are discussed in root exudates and root glucosides.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: s8h mutants exude scopoletin but no further oxidized coumarins and have impaired iron nutrition at high pH.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports S8H as a necessary conversion step toward catecholic coumarins, but does not explicitly describe it as a committed or key control step.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results show strong induction under iron deficiency in roots, promoter activity along primary/lateral roots except apical zones, and strongest expression in epidermis.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: qPCR data in Results show At3g12900 expression is strongly induced in iron-deficient roots.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Studied under iron deficiency and elevated pH conditions.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Supported by T-DNA mutants, qPCR, promoter-GUS/GFP, N. benthamiana reconstitution, and coumarin rescue experiments.

Omics_Data.reason: Comparative metabolomics and transcriptome mining are explicitly described without accessions.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods state the S8H promoter sequence used for reporters was 2023 bp.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: No physical interactions with F6’H1 or CYP82C4 are shown; these are pathway partners only.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: s8h mutants show chlorosis and reduced chlorophyll/Fe under alkaline low-Fe conditions.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper does not explicitly make this gene-specific engineering recommendation.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results explicitly state active recombinant protein could not readily be obtained because of instability and function was established through genetics/reconstitution.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summary points are directly supported by Results.

corrections.reason: No physical interactions with F6’H1 or CYP82C4 are shown; these are pathway partners only.

corrections.old_value: Strong engineering target for increasing catecholic coumarin output and improving iron uptake, especially together with export machinery; preferable to preserve fraxetin production under alkaline soils
corrections.reason: The paper does not explicitly make this gene-specific engineering recommendation.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Results and Methods identify CYP82C4 as At4g31940.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Mutants are in Col-0 background unless otherwise noted; cyp82C4 is not excepted.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana was the applied species for mutant and reporter studies.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: cyp82C4 lines are in Col-0 background.

Enzyme_Name.reason: Results describe CYP82C4 as encoding a cytochrome P450 enzyme.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Cytochrome P450 enzyme is explicit; CYP82 family identity is inherent in the gene name CYP82C4.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The paper uses yeast microsomal fractions expressing CYP82C4, but does not explicitly state the native subcellular localization as 'microsome'.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: CYP82C4 is the final enzyme in sideretin/catecholic coumarin biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Results show CYP82C4 catalyzes the terminal sideretin-forming oxidation step.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: It is explicitly the final step in the pathway to compounds 1 and 2/sideretin.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Fraxetin accumulates in cyp82C4 mutants and CYP82C4 microsomes convert fraxetin to products identical to 1 and 2.

Primary_Product.reason: Results infer and then confirm CYP82C4 yields sideretin (compound 2, 5-hydroxyfraxetin).

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Supported by mutant accumulation of fraxetin, in vitro microsome assay, and chemical identity of sideretin/quinone pair.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: CYP82C4 directly forms the target end product sideretin.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Sideretin is the direct product of CYP82C4 action.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Sideretin is a coumarin derived from fraxetin/scopoletin pathway.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: Sideretin is identified as a redox-active exuded coumarin involved in iron mobilization.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Sideretin is in root exudates and its glucosides in roots.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: cyp82C4 mutants lack sideretin and accumulate fraxetin; at high pH they are not impaired and can appear more robust than wild type.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports it as the terminal determinant, but does not discuss rate limitation or formal control-step status.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results show strong induction in iron-deficient roots, root-localized promoter activity except apical zones, and strongest expression in epidermis.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: qPCR confirms strong induction in iron-deficient roots.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Iron deficiency induction and pH-dependent functional consequences are directly discussed; sideretin predominates at low pH and fraxetin may be advantageous at high pH.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are explicitly used: T-DNA mutants, qPCR, yeast microsomes, N. benthamiana reconstitution, promoter reporters, and phenotyping.

Omics_Data.reason: Comparative metabolomics and transcriptome mining are explicitly described with no accession provided.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods state CYP82C4 promoter length used for reporter constructs was 1151 bp.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: No physical interaction with S8H or F6’H1 is demonstrated.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results explicitly suggest CYP82C4-mediated conversion to sideretin may be maladaptive at high pH and mutants can outperform wild type.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This gene-specific engineering guidance is not explicitly proposed in the paper.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results show CYP82C4 loss in Eutrema salsugineum correlates with absence of sideretin production relative to Brassica rapa.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three summary points are supported by the Results.

corrections.reason: No physical interaction with S8H or F6’H1 is demonstrated.

corrections.old_value: Useful as a pH-context-dependent engineering target; deployment may favor low-pH environments, whereas reducing CYP82C4 activity could preserve fraxetin for alkaline soils
corrections.reason: This gene-specific engineering guidance is not explicitly proposed in the paper.
